speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three. The survivor is Mania Lichtenstein. Today is April 1st, 1996. We were just starting to talk about the ghetto, and that there were two ghettos actually set up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give us a little bit more description about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Again, the-- the idea of the two ghettos was a trick behind it. They set up two ghettos. One, they called the Living Ghetto. The other one, the Dead Ghetto. The Living Ghetto was for people who are workers, who have a piece of paper saying they a shoemaker, they a tailor, they a bricklayer, they a-- they a whatever. Even a doctor. Somebody who would come-- come in handy. The others were designated death. They were useless. They had no-- no trade. Because a lot didn't have trades, you know. They were shopkeepers or whatever they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were these two ghettos. The Living Ghetto was the bigger one. They put barbed wire around them. We were not allowed anymore to leave the ghetto, unless taken out for-- for forced labor. But they needed us. Even the Dead Ghetto, they were taken out. Because why not make use of you while they let you live? Immediately we had to make up-- we had to wear on our sleeves, a white band with a star of David. But that wasn't very-- it wasn't very obvious, like, you know, you have-- they have to spot you from afar. We were ordered to remove this, and I couldn't remember-- I tried last night-- how did we get these orange-- gigantic, orange-- gigantic, orange patches? Like a sun, in the front and in the back. So even if you ran off or somewhere, you are spotted immediately.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like two suns. And we had to wear, young or old, unless you were a baby in a crib. You know, otherwise, you couldn't walk. If you-- if you could walk, you had to wear it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember more about what the patch looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just a plain--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have the Star of David?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was enough. The yellow was so bright, like, you would spot it for miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it circular?

speaker: interviewee
text: A circular-- round, perfect round circle. Everybody, bright yellow. It actually was an orangey-yellow. Like-- leave it to the Germans. Good enough to be very efficient for the purpose. So we wore-- we already paraded around, branded, you know, behind barbed wire. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not-- nowhere to go. I can only talk from my perspective. I was young and very romantic.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: 17, you know, maybe 16. No, more 17. Romantic, and also music, as I, I still say, music flows in my veins. And people just sit and sing, sitting around the kitchen table. My three-- the three sisters, and sing and cry. Tears were rolling down, because it's very sentimental. You were young and you wanted to live. And it was I remember, spring, you know. But you were not a normal-- you were not a human being. You-- you were-- you were not allowed to go for a walk outside, just in the ghetto. So you had to do something. Days were long. Food wasn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: I think it's time for another song. Do you remember any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no, oh my God, don't make me sing. Ah, the Polish songs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any others that you remember, specifically, in the ghetto, that you sang?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what, when I saw I cried a lot. Not because of the story-- OK, because of the story-- but that doesn't compare even, to what we've been through. Because there, it was a positive thing, a good German who saved Jews. But there was that Polish cabaret, and that Polish songstress sings. She-- she [INAUDIBLE] uses him. And she sings, and I cried. [SINGING IN POLISH] And so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this song-- every song was so sentimental. And of course, you had a heart full, because, you were young. My mother, of course, was dwindling. She-- she knew-- I mean, when you look at your children, your family, you know they're going to slaughter them soon. You know? And we were just not paying attention, like, it's just that they clipped our wings, you know? This is it. You just stay in this place. And so we would sing and cry. So anyway, the days were-- they-- they put us, like, behind this barbed wire, except for when they took us out to do some-- it was, after all, the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were bombed-out houses. We had to remove debris. I mean, big workers we were. But you-- you did what they-- what they told you to. Also, the men, I remember, they used to say they are building a cable. I don't even know where, you know, like I wasn't-- all I know is that they took out men. And immediately, they gave us a foretaste of what-- who we are and what is going to happen. Immediately, shooting right in the middle of the street. Once they took out about, I don't know, a lot of people. I don't want to quote something I'm not sure. But I don't know, maybe 100. And shot them right there just to show the town what-- what is there to expect. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we sit in this ghetto, and every time you hear stories. There was one in-- very infamous story. On Yom Kippur night, couldn't-- couldn't choose a more appropriate night, you know, the Jew Yom Kippur night, where he's supposed to be in temple. They took 200 Jews, took them to the-- to the jail, to the town jail. And they never came out. Then we found that they tortured them, they shot them. This was just a foreplay, that didn't mean anything. But already, we knew. It was called the Black Friday, you know, Friday night of Yom Kippur of all times.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is what was a-- a prevalent occurrence. Doesn't matter, shoot them. Or a Jew with a long-- you know, there was a Hasidic Jew, with a long payots. They would chase them in the street, make them sing. Cut off-- you know, there's even pictures-- I have a book.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Pincus ?] Ludmir. Ludmir was a [INAUDIBLE] and they gathered-- after the war, in Israel, they printed. So they show how the Jew and the German laughing, they're standing and laughing, with a scissor cutting his payots, you know, his be-- sideburns. But I don't know. You are American. You might not maybe know so much the significance. But this is what was like, your-- this was part of you. So they were mocking and laughing, and, you know. So this is what was happening. And by then, we already well knew that this ain't a joke. [LAUGHS] There were people coming from neighboring towns. I remember one young boy who came. He ran away because first, where my father worked in that pharmacy, they-- this was a small town. I don't know how many people, but much, much less [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: So they liquidated the town. He escaped, and he came and he started saying, Jews! You don't know what's happening, they're slaughtering everybody. So I just remember, we said, oh what a liar. What are you talking about? Make up stories.

speaker: interviewee
text: You just didn't want to believe, you know. You refused to believe. But this is what was happening, and by that time, we already knew that, hey, this is it. No more fooling yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get food to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food-- whatever we had. At the end, it was bad. It was starvation, already. The extermination was our salvation. Because-- because they didn't give us, or we didn't go out. Whatever we had in the ghetto, that was-- and as I say, a Pole would come up to-- to the fence. And in all his life, he would-- in those days, he wouldn't see a bar of soap. But perfumed soap, I mean, that was to him like-- and he would give you a potato, or a little bit of something. And I wrote several things, and in one, I write, like, I cried before. I wish I had told my mother more how I adored her, how I revered her, you know. What a-- what a hero she was. But you know, you're a kid, and it's natural, until it's too late. And I remember, it was winter. It was so cold. There was no fuel also. Cold in the house. The windows-- our winter were not like a winter that you know, even coming from the East. The windows were all covered with beautiful designs. I don't know if you ever saw the winter-- the fro-- the freeze on the window. Oh, I used to look at it and fantasize. Beautiful, like, you know, the snowflakes created. Cold. We couldn't get out. The kids couldn't go out of bed. They would cover-- like only our nose, maybe, to breathe. And my mother standing over the stove. That was already, like, before the pogroms, it was already the end of-- of this ghetto-- cooking a soup for us, a potato with some water. But it was the best thing I ever ate, of course. It was hot. It enabled us to get out of bed, because you couldn't get out, it was so cold. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get along with the other people that were staying in your house? I-- I really don't-- you know, it's so many things erased from my mind. Also my grandmother, who lived with my mother's brother, which you'll see a picture later of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I used to live with them. But when the gathering started, when they started putting you all in one-- one basket, my grandmother lived with us. And I don't know. There was no question how, or did you like it, or what did you do? We were not-- We were people already deprived. I mean, things that are so terribly important in life become so-- it's nothing. Frivolous. It's nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- it's funny. In the face of death, you perceive everything so differently. Those things that are so terribly important stop existing, like they're nonsense.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you develop any new friendships with the people that came into your house and lived with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no no. I don't know. I don't even remember their faces. I don't know who they were. It didn't last very long, because as soon as everything was ready, they had every-- everyone, as they say, in one basket-- the pogrom-- and-- and everything is gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to the pogrom, you had mentioned there were two ghettos. Did each one have a different name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No names. Yeah. Yeah, actually. The [SPEAKING YIDDISH], in Yiddish. The Dead and the Living, yes. This is what they called them.

words.text: This is what they called them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the people themselves know which one they were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. [INAUDIBLE] how did I-- did we happen to be in the Living Ghetto? Our house was in the-- my sister, you know, they also organized a Judenrat, it was called. Like a Jewish office to take care of internal needs, there, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was working there. And she produced, for my father-- are we now on? For my father a piece of paper stating that he's a bricklayer. And this is how. A bricklayer, OK, you're allowed to stay in the ghetto. It didn't mean much to them, anyway, because they just tricked, you know? So the others knew that they have nothing to hold on to. And these people thought-- actually it had a purpose, why they did it, which I'll mention later. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we stayed in that. And the other people just knew they're doomed, you know. And each ghetto had a separate barbed-wire fence around it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: A separate fence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was one ghetto from another ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, the city wasn't like-- when you had to go somewhere, you had to go by foot. Or if you went to the railway station, at [INAUDIBLE], you know, like-- what do you call it-- a [FRENCH], you know, in French. A [FRENCH] with horses. Fancy, with the horse and the-- not wagon, but, you know, like you see in those old movies. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it was not very far, but yet, far.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any communication between the two ghettos?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Unless you were taken to work and you met the others, so you talked. Otherwise, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anyone that was in the other ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I mean, that was a city that people-- my school, my-- you know. You knew a lot of people. It wasn't like the big city. And not even New York, but even a smaller city, that you might not even know your neighbors. No, this, everybody knew everybody, almost.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give us an idea about how many people were in each one, each ghetto? Approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, actually, I said before that the Dead Ghetto was larger. No, the other one was larger. Yeah. Because I remember now, the area, it extended much further.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of the streets that were the borders of each ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like your ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well. We were, like, near the river. The others were-- no. I can't really-- I can't. There's so much. I never took the pain to write it. It wasn't important, you know, where or when. It was just happening. And now you just have to recollect everything, try to.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you keep track of the Jewish holidays while you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: As much as-- oh, yeah, you knew. I don't know, how do they keep track any-- any-- any time? They probably had some kind of-- yeah, they had a calendar. Oh yeah, they--

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were still religious observances in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. You still tried as much as you could. I mean, there wasn't any fancy food or-- we went too, too long. I don't remember, like within a year, maybe-- yeah, that the extermination. They weren't-- they weren't going to keep us, entertain us in the ghetto. You know, once they had us cooped up, we were ripe for the slaughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was happening outside of the ghetto? Except for the story that you were told by this young person from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that wasn't outside. He came--

speaker: interviewer
text: He came into the ghetto to tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came into the ghetto, or was it even-- no, it was even before. I really don't-- it's so difficult to remember exactly. All I-- that was already happening, the slaughtering. So he must have been in the ghetto, because he was the one to say, OK, you don't believe me? You'll see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you still have your radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no,no. This was something. When we were-- as soon as we came into the ghetto, a proclamation was given, all valuables must be brought-- returned to the German. There was set up a place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Radio, silver, gold, money, anything of value. And who cared? Like I say in my story, how pathetic it was seeing those, bent under the heavy load, bringing all the valuables, with-- with no even wincing, you know, with no even regret. It didn't mean anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't mean anything, all the gold and all the-- but, you know, there were heirloom given from generation to generation. You know, people didn't go out and buy a new candelabra or a new something. It was different times, you know. There were other priorities, to-- to buy, to maintain the well-being of your family, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father try to hide any valuables?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe. I don't know. Kids were never included in any big decisions. And this especially was-- probably would have been a secret, because nobody should know what--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a feeling that they might have. But then again, I later came back to that place. But I-- I'll have to relate about it later, what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned you had a certain work assignment to-- while you were in this ghetto. Can you tell us more about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not In this ghetto. It's not like workers-- they took us wherever they needed us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cleaning out the debris from the homes of, you know. The one thing that is very important, because it dictated the fate of my life. At the end, three days before the pogrom-- they had it all planned, you know? Three days before the pogrom, they took 500 young women, ages approximately maybe 15 years old to maybe 30-- I don't know exactly-- young women, young able women, to work in the fields.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was Ukraine, it was harvest time. The end-- the last few days in August. And fields had to be tended, right? So why not? Jewish women knew about field work, like-- like, you know, they knew-- they knew about, nowadays, science. But you have to go. They treated us terribly.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we-- we worked on that field and under the blazing sun nonstop. There was such a storm, a rainstorm, a hurricane, almost. Thunder and lightning. And you have no idea how-- when I was a kid-- how afraid we were of thunder and lightning. And we had to be out in the open field, not even hide. The rain pouring on us. Work in the field. We were not allowed to walk. We were supposed to run with the bundles. I never found out exactly, in English, what you call them. Because in Ukraine, you see these bundles of wheat. You tied them in the field when it's done, when it's harvested. I'll still have to find-- certain things I have to find out. And we had to do-- work-- all under the machine that was grinding the-- the wheat. And there was a German and a horse, swift horse, with a long whip. And whipping. Whipping left and right, so you were just running and trying to escape him. I even got a slap on my face. I mean now, I could never tolerate. I mean, maybe I would if-- no, I'm not in the position to say anything-- but I would just die right on the spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then anything they-- they did to you, it was, what else do you expect? He thought that I'm putting too much of those bundles in the machine. You were feeding on top of the machine. He thought-- excuse me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I would never do anything that I'm not supposed to. But this is what he thought, the German. And he gave me a slap up on my cheek, like stars were flying. That's a terrible feeling, you know, when you get-- but anyway, you did not feel very important anyway, like you amounted to nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember crying from the pain?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think-- I don't think you were even could afford such a luxury. You know, crying, laughing, hey, these are luxuries. You were just a number to be slaughtered soon.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, the first day, all they did is-- a wagon-- a horse and wagon came and brought us soup and a drink of water in mid-day. The first day and the second day. The third day, no soup, no water. Because they knew that the following morning, we are going to be eliminated, so why even bother, you know? Why bother, already, giving them a drink of water? And here, I don't know what the temperature was, but our winters were extremely severe. Our summers were hot. Very dry heat. Very nice, but hot. And in the open field, you needed a drink of water. So there was a frog-infested pond. I remember that little pond. We had a lot of ponds, you know? I don't see them here. And we drank from that pond.

speaker: interviewee
text: What is a very moving incident to me, when I went-- that was the first day. When I went with the 500 young women to work in the field, my grandmother-- oh, she was such a proper, proper lady-- she-- she gave me-- we didn't have, already, food.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the end. As I say, the extermination was the solution. You know? Everything will be OK now. She got-- somebody gave her a little sour pickle, and she gave it to me to take along. I write-- I wrote about it in my story, that this little pickle-- it might sound comical to you, funny, stupid, maybe. There was nothing else to eat, you know, except for that bit of soup that they gave you there. And she gave me that little pickle. And I thought that this was so her-- heroic. Because she could have eaten it herself. Why did she give it to me? That pickle. I cannot tell you what that pickle-- little, litle pickle-- I remember that little-- and I'm not even, you see a cake, or as I say, a sweet tooth. They had good means. But the pickle, I was not for salty things.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that pickle, at that time was so symbolic. It was symbolic of her love to-- to sacrifice-- to give it, to offer it to me instead of eating it herself. Anyway, then came the third day. Nothing was given. We were treated already like animals. Chasing us all over the field. And we worked until it was pitch-dark. Probably they started gathering us up, to take us back, about 10:00 at night. And two, three-- three trucks came. As tight as possible, you know, the animals. And like the horses-- not horses, but-- it's like cattle. Cattle trucks. And they took us back to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now here, it's already 11:00, which was very late for me. I mean, I'm not home yet, you know? God knows what my family's thinking. Knowing anyway, in the air, it was all spelled out. This is it, folks. And they bring us, not to the Living Ghetto, but to the Dead Ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I go to the gate. At the gate, the Ukrainians-- Ukrainians were terrific for the Germans, their right hand. The Germans didn't even need to do much, because the Ukrainians did it with pleasure. You know, with pleasure, not as a duty. When I come to the gate and I start crying. You know, I'm afraid. I'm afraid of the dark. I was afraid of a fly, anything. Very-- not very brave child. And I begged them, I say, I live in the other ghetto. Please let me go home.

speaker: interviewee
text: They laughed. They pushed me, and they laughed, oh, go away. You know, like they knew what was going to happen, you know? They laughed and they don't let me go. And I can just picture my mother, tearing her hair out. The baby is not home yet. I must have been killed already. You must have killed me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was true. Later on, somebody who survived that other ghetto told me what was going on in our home. I mean, I wasn't home. It was already, when I came, 11:00, it's midnight. I'm not home yet, so I must be gone already. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your other sisters at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I was the only one from them to send to work in the fields. From the fields, they bring us to the Dead Ghetto. I don't know what to do. It's pitch dark. I'm afraid of the air. My sister's in-laws lived in-- in the Dead Ghetto. So I say, OK, I'll go to them for the night. Tomorrow, we'll see what happens. I come to them. Of course, everybody was alert. Everybody-- nobody undresses, nobody goes to sleep. Everybody knows. It's so funny. It was, like, in the air. It was so different there. It was so quiet. Dead quiet. Explicitly dead quiet. There was like nothing stirs. Everybody just knew, this is it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to them and stayed with them. We had-- it was a building-- like, this was quite a prominent family lived there. And as I say, they put people together, so they happened to be in that building. It was a four-story building. There weren't many high-rise in our city then, you know? A very prominent-- [? Lemoyic's ?] the name. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's a long night. We stay dressed. Nobody goes to sleep. Nobody even dozes off. You sit and you wait. But then, so many hours, and you get restless. Let's go down or up, to see what the others are doing, because on each floor there were people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was at dawn. You know, nothing is happening. It's still, it's quiet. Nobody talks, nobody cries, nobody-- nobody says anything. Everybody knows very well, and has heard, what is going to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stay in one floor, like my sister's in-laws and some extended family of theirs. Usually families try to stay together when they were, like, relocated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You get restless. OK, we'll go and see what the other families are doing. We ran up the steps to the other floor. And this is already, as I say, dawn. It started-- you could see already, before it was very dark. We looked down, and the ghetto is surrounded by Germans and Ukrainians, rifles ready, like this. And then we knew, aha, this is it.
